cluster_name: US airstrikes hit Iran bridges sixth night; Hormuz shipping near-halt
tldr: Across all 27 coverage regions, headline writers collapse this story to one dominant frame: a bilateral security exchange in which the US strikes Iran for a sixth night and Iran retaliates across the Gulf. A secondary economic disruption layer surfaces only in Turkish, Indian, Chinese, and Egyptian headlines spotlighting the near-shutdown of Strait of Hormuz shipping. Diplomatic failure and Trump strategy critique appear primarily in US, opinion-magazine, South African, and Japanese outlets, while civilian-targeting condemnation is confined to Pakistani, West African, and Iranian state headlines — a moral sub-frame almost entirely absent at the headline level in Western press despite its prominence in body text.
event_summary: The US struck Iranian bridges and an airport for the sixth night on 17 July 2026, killing at least seven in Hormozgan province. Iran fired at US bases in Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan, Syria, and Oman; the Strait of Hormuz remained blocked to all but three vessels in 24 hours.

frames.sub_frame: six nights of mutual US-Iran strikes

single_outlet_findings.finding: The only UK or Western outlet to embed Trump's triumphalist self-assessment ('We are winning big in Iran') directly in the headline, foregrounding US presidential confidence rather than military facts or casualties — a framing absent from BBC, Guardian, and Independent headlines covering the same night.

Publishes the only metaphorical headline in the 27-country corpus: 'Missiles fired in Hormuz, ballots shaken in America' — the sole outlet to link the military conflict to US domestic political disruption at the homepage level, a rhetorical register entirely absent from all other 52 outlets.

The only headline in the corpus to foreground the Houthi/Red Sea escalation threat as its primary frame ('Iran Looking To Unleash Houthis On Another Critical Waterway'), a dimension found in several body texts but suppressed to headline silence by every other outlet across all 27 regions.

The only outlet in the corpus to attach a specific dollar figure to the campaign's cost in a headline ('military costs soar toward $100 bln'), framing the war through financial liability rather than military exchange — a register entirely absent from Western headline layers.

bottom_line: At the headline layer, the story is overwhelmingly a security-exchange frame — six nights of mutual US-Iran strikes across the Gulf — while the economic shock to Hormuz shipping, diplomatic collapse, and civilian-targeting debate visible in body text are suppressed to secondary or absent status in the vast majority of the 27 coverage regions.
